ad_copy: 22 years ago, a woman in Rhode Island asked: what if jewelry meant something? It still does.
brand_visual_direction: Sustainability is not a footnote — it is the visual argument. This variation explicitly surfaces the material origins of every piece: recycled copper, reclaimed metal, conscious craft. The color grade runs cooler and more elemental than the heritage variation — earthy greens, raw copper oxide tones, the grey-green of a New England overcast sky. Text overlays carry specific sustainability facts mid-film. The edit is deliberate but the eco framing creates a secondary narrative layer that runs parallel to the founding story. The final card includes an explicit sustainability line beneath the logo.
generation_prompt: Cinematic eco-conscious brand origin film, 16:9, earthy and elemental color grade — raw copper oxide green, warm terracotta, overcast Rhode Island grey-white sky. Opens on extreme close-up of a copper wire being bent between bare hands — macro lens, high detail, the oxidized surface of the copper visible, fingerprints pressed into the metal. Silence except the metallic whisper of bending wire. Camera pulls back to a craftsperson at a long worktable in a brick workshop — morning light through tall windows, Attitude of Gratitude bangle worn naturally on their wrist. Cut to brick building exterior under an overcast sky. Text overlay: 'Cranston, Rhode Island. 2004.' Minimal acoustic guitar begins. A woman reads a meaning card — private, unposed. Overhead: bangles, copper wire, and meaning cards on a worktable. Close-up insert: a small printed label reading 'Recycled Metals' partially visible among the materials. Montage: five wrists in motion — kitchen, car, garden, desk, morning coffee — Token of Love and Glass Butterfly bangles among stacked pieces. Brief insert: a pile of raw copper wire and reclaimed metal before it becomes jewelry. Return to workshop: craftsperson holds Glass Butterfly bangle to window light — light scatters across wood. Three intimate wrist close-ups — Make Your Own Luck Ladybug bangle visible. Ends on a dark textured recycled copper surface — Alex and Ani logo in gold, 'Est. 2004', and a single line beneath: 'Made from recycled metals. Made to last.' Silence.

scenes.camera_angle: Slightly overhead — looking down at hands from above, wire across center frame
scenes.framing: Wire fills frame. The oxidized surface and fingerprint impressions dominate. Copper's natural patina is intentionally visible.
scenes.key_light: Overcast window light — flat, even, emphasizing texture over drama
scenes.light_quality: Diffused, cool-neutral — the kind of light that makes material surfaces read clearly
scenes.light_mood: Elemental. Material. Before the story — just the stuff of the world.
scenes.subject: A single recycled copper wire being bent between bare hands
scenes.action: Deliberate bend. The copper's texture and patina are the focus — this is about material, not mystery.
scenes.background: Worktable surface — close, blurred, earthy brown
scenes.depth_of_field: Extremely shallow — wire surface in razor focus, natural oxidation visible at macro level
scenes.color_palette: Raw copper, oxide green, warm terracotta, dark brown
scenes.color_grade: Cooler and more elemental than the heritage variation — copper's natural color preserved, not warmed artificially

scenes.shot_type: Wide pull-back to full workshop interior; cut to building exterior under overcast sky
scenes.camera_angle: Eye-level pull-back. Exterior: slightly low angle, sky dominant in frame.
scenes.camera_movement: Slow deliberate pull-back from Scene 1. Exterior: static, held for 3 seconds.
scenes.framing: Craftsperson at worktable with overcast window light. Exterior: building fills left two-thirds, grey-white sky right and above.
scenes.key_light: Overcast morning light through tall windows — even, cool-warm, no strong shadows
scenes.light_quality: Soft and directional — New England overcast quality, not harsh
scenes.light_mood: Considered. Workmanlike. The seriousness of a workshop, not a studio.
scenes.subject: Craftsperson at worktable; brick building exterior in Cranston, RI under overcast sky
scenes.action: Craftsperson works with hands. Attitude of Gratitude bangle visible on wrist — incidental. Exterior: static, the building's age and materiality visible.
scenes.expression: Focused on work. No camera awareness.
scenes.background: Brick walls, timber, tall paned windows — natural workshop environment. Exterior: aged brick, New England sky.
scenes.depth_of_field: Medium — craftsperson sharp, workshop context readable
scenes.color_palette: Warm brick, grey-white sky, cool-warm window light, aged timber
scenes.color_grade: Slightly desaturated — cooler than heritage version. Sky retains its cool grey-white.
scenes.voiceover: There was a question she kept asking.
scenes.music: Single acoustic guitar fingerpick — sparse, tentative.
scenes.text_overlay: Cranston, Rhode Island. 2004. — small serif, warm gold, lower third. Fades in on exterior cut.

scenes.shot_type: Medium close-up on woman's face; overhead flat lay with insert cut
scenes.camera_angle: Eye-level on face. Overhead directly above worktable. Insert: straight-on close-up of recycled materials label.
scenes.camera_movement: Face: static with barely perceptible micro-drift. Overhead: static. Insert: static.
scenes.framing: Face left of center. Overhead: worktable edge to edge. Insert: label partially visible among materials — not foregrounded.
scenes.key_light: Soft window light, slight side angle
scenes.light_mood: Private. Interior. The weight of a real question.
scenes.subject: Woman reading meaning card; overhead of bangles, wire, meaning cards; brief insert of recycled metals materials label among worktable objects
scenes.action: She reads. A quiet shift in expression. Overhead reveals the full environment. Insert acknowledges the material origin.
scenes.background: Soft warm blur behind face. Worktable surface for overhead.
scenes.depth_of_field: Shallow on face. Even across overhead.
scenes.color_palette: Skin tones, warm ivory, raw copper, aged paper, oxide green
scenes.color_grade: Slightly cooler than Scene 2 — more naturalistic, less amber
scenes.voiceover: What if a piece of jewelry could carry something real? Not just gold. Not just silver. Something you'd actually feel.
scenes.music: Acoustic guitar, single melodic phrase — 3 to 4 notes.
scenes.text_overlay: Brief lower-third text on insert cut: 'Recycled metals. Since 2004.' — small, clean, sans-serif, white. Holds for 2 seconds.

scenes.shot_type: Montage — five wrist shots plus one brief insert of raw copper wire material
scenes.camera_angle: Varied per cut — observational, handheld quality
scenes.camera_movement: Loose, natural, follows gesture. One cut: a brief static insert of raw copper wire coiled in a workshop bin — deliberate eco beat.
scenes.framing: Wrists in context of activity. Insert: copper wire fills frame — a visual callback to Scene 1 but at scale.
scenes.key_light: Varied per location — kitchen, car, garden, desk, café. All naturally motivated.
scenes.light_quality: Each shot is its own environment. Consistency is warmth, not sameness.
scenes.light_mood: Alive. Present. Plural. These are different lives, different places — the same intention.
scenes.subject: Five women in motion. Token of Love and Glass Butterfly bangles among stacked wrists. One cut: raw copper wire before it becomes jewelry.
scenes.action: Natural gestures throughout. The copper wire insert is 1.5 seconds — a material reminder inside the montage.
scenes.expression: Not looking at camera. In their own moments.
scenes.background: Varied: kitchen, car, garden, desk, café. Copper wire insert: workshop bin or worktable.
scenes.color_palette: Warm amber, car-window blue, garden green-gold, desk neutral, café cream, raw copper
scenes.color_grade: Cooler unifying grade across all cuts — slightly desaturated compared to heritage version. The eco palette: more earth, less gold.
scenes.music: Second guitar layer enters — counter-melody, soft.
scenes.text_overlay: Three separate lower-thirds across this scene's duration: '22 years.' — '66 countries.' — '19 languages.' Each appears briefly, one at a time, in sync with voiceover. Small serif, warm gold.

scenes.shot_type: Medium close-up — hand holding bangle to window; hold on light scatter
scenes.camera_angle: Slightly low angle — hand raised toward window, light source above and behind
scenes.camera_movement: Slow push toward the scattered light on the worktable surface — minimal, felt rather than seen.
scenes.framing: Hand and Glass Butterfly bangle in upper frame. Light scatter across worktable below — constellation of refracted points.
scenes.key_light: Hard morning window light through the glass beads — the bangle itself is the light modifier
scenes.light_quality: Point-source through translucent material — precise, prismatic, natural
scenes.light_mood: Revelation through material. The eco argument made visual: the raw materials become something that scatters light.
scenes.subject: Craftsperson's hand holding Glass Butterfly beaded charm bangle to tall window
scenes.action: Bangle raised slowly. Light scatters across wood surface. Camera holds on the light points for 2 full seconds.
scenes.background: Window: overexposed white. Worktable: warm amber grain.
scenes.depth_of_field: Split — bangle slightly soft, light scatter on worktable in sharp focus
scenes.color_palette: Overcast white window, warm amber wood, scattered prismatic light, copper
scenes.color_grade: Cooler than heritage Scene 5 — the window light reads more silver-white than golden
scenes.voiceover: She just wanted to make something that meant something.

scenes.camera_angle: One from above, one eye-level, one slight low angle
scenes.camera_movement: First: static. Second: barely perceptible drift. Third: static, slow fade begins.
scenes.framing: Wrist and hand — enough human context, not a product shot.
scenes.key_light: Soft, varied — window light, ambient indoor, natural outdoor shade
scenes.light_quality: Intimate, observed, not lit for a studio
scenes.light_mood: Settled. Quiet. These women carry these pieces the way they carry anything that matters.
scenes.subject: Three women, three ages. Make Your Own Luck Ladybug bangle visible on one wrist. Stacked bangles on others.
scenes.action: One glances at wrist. One adjusts a bangle — habitual. One holds wrist in her lap, still.
scenes.background: Soft domestic and personal spaces — warm, indistinct.
scenes.depth_of_field: Shallow — wrists in focus, backgrounds fall away
scenes.color_palette: Skin tones, warm gold, silver, ambient softness
scenes.color_grade: Slightly neutral — grounding after Scene 5's light moment. Warm shadows held.
scenes.music: Guitar fades to single held note.

scenes.shot_type: Static brand card on textured recycled metal surface
scenes.framing: Logo centered. Two lines of text beneath. Textured surface fills frame completely.
scenes.key_light: Ambient only — surface absorbs light, not reflects it
scenes.light_quality: Flat, absorbed — the texture is the subject
scenes.light_mood: Complete quiet. The weight of 22 years in a surface.
scenes.subject: Alex and Ani logo in warm gold. 'Est. 2004.' below. Third line: 'Made from recycled metals. Made to last.'
scenes.action: Nothing moves. Three-second hold in absolute silence.
scenes.background: Dark textured recycled copper surface — deep, warm, almost tactile. The material argument in a single texture.
scenes.color_palette: Deep warm black, raw copper undertones, warm gold
scenes.color_grade: Darkest grade of the film. The recycled metal surface should feel physical, almost 3D in its texture.
scenes.text_overlay: Alex and Ani logo — centered, warm gold. 'Est. 2004.' — small, below. 'Made from recycled metals. Made to last.' — smaller still, below that, same warm gold. No animation.

style_references: Patagonia 'Don't Buy This Jacket' campaign — sustainability as the primary creative argument, not a footnote
Eileen Fisher brand films — material origins, slow fashion, anti-trend messaging
New England documentary photography — overcast light, honest materiality
Industrial process films — the beauty of raw materials before transformation
negative_prompts: Tropical, sun-soaked, or warm-golden color grades that obscure the eco-material palette — this variation intentionally reads cooler and more elemental
Any visual cliche associated with fast-fashion or trend advertising: speed, flash, abundance, newness-worship
Clean, clinical, or artificially perfect surfaces — all material imperfection is intentional and must be preserved
Jewelry held toward camera or product-presented in any shot other than Scene 5's window light moment
Morphing hands, extra fingers, distorted skin at macro level, or unnatural metal surface artifacts in close-up shots
Sustainability messaging that feels like greenwashing — no leaf icons, no overt 'eco' visual clichés
Any color treatment that makes the recycled copper surface in Scene 7 read as new, shiny, or aspirationally luxurious
post_production_notes: Edit rhythm: Slightly more breathing room than the heritage version — an extra half-second on the material inserts (copper wire in Scene 4, recycled label in Scene 3) to let the eco beats land without feeling rushed.

Color grade: The LUT should preserve copper's natural oxide green undertones in Scene 1 rather than warming them artificially. The palette is earthy and elemental, not golden and aspirational. Cooler shadow base throughout versus the heritage variation.
Text overlays (Scene 4): The three stat lower-thirds — '22 years.', '66 countries.', '19 languages.' — should match the voiceover cadence exactly. Brief, precise, no lingering.

Sustainability text (Scene 3 insert): 'Recycled metals. Since 2004.' must appear only on the insert cut of the materials label — never overlaid on talent faces or wrist shots. Clean, minimal, sans-serif.

Final card (Scene 7): The third line of text — 'Made from recycled metals. Made to last.' — should be noticeably smaller than 'Est. 2004.' but still legible. This is a statement, not a tagline. It should not look like marketing copy.

Music mix: Acoustic guitar is slightly more restrained than the heritage version — it should feel like it belongs to the New England overcast atmosphere, not the golden-hour warmth of heritage variation. Single-mic recording preferred over a produced studio sound.

Scene 5 light scatter: The prismatic scatter should read silver-white due to the cooler grade — not warm golden as in the heritage version. This is a subtle but important distinction between the two variations.

Sound design: In Scene 1 and during the copper wire insert in Scene 4, the ambient sound of the workshop — the faint sound of metal, of physical making — should be present beneath the music at very low level. This reinforces the material-reality argument.

scene_2.treatment: Worn naturally on craftsperson's wrist — visible but incidental to the workshop reveal.

scene_3.treatment: Environmental overhead — among meaning cards and copper wire. Recycled materials label partially visible nearby.

scene_4.product: Token of Love Charm Bangle, Glass Butterfly Beaded Charm Bangle
scene_4.treatment: Among stacked bangles in wrist montage. Never isolated. Raw copper wire insert provides material context.

scene_5.treatment: Held to window — the light scatter is the eco-craft argument made visual: raw material transformed into something that scatters light.

scene_6.product: Make Your Own Luck Ladybug Charm Bangle
scene_6.treatment: Visible on one wrist among three — not featured, not presented.

sustainability_notes: This variation makes the sustainability argument explicitly through three channels: (1) visual — copper's natural oxidation and raw material inserts surface the material origin; (2) textual — 'Recycled metals. Since 2004.' in Scene 3 and 'Made from recycled metals. Made to last.' on the final card; (3) editorial — the copper wire insert in Scene 4 creates a material throughline from raw wire (Scene 1) to finished piece (Scene 5) to worn jewelry (Scene 6). The argument is: this is not fast fashion. This is a single wire, 22 years, and a question that still matters.
